recovery_position: Kneel beside the person and ensure the airway is open (tilt head back, lift chin).
Place the arm nearest you at a right angle to the body, elbow bent, palm facing up.
Bring the far arm across the chest and hold the back of that hand against the nearest cheek.
With your other hand, pull the far knee up until the foot is flat on the ground.
Keeping the hand against the cheek, pull on the bent knee to roll the person onto their side toward you.
Adjust the top leg so both the hip and knee are at right angles.
Tilt the head back gently to keep the airway open; the hand under the cheek keeps the airway aligned.
Call 911 (or local emergency number); monitor breathing continuously until help arrives.
If breathing stops at any point, begin CPR immediately.

adult: Confirm the scene is safe; check the person — tap shoulders and shout 'Are you okay?'
Call 911 (or have a bystander call) and request an AED if available.
Lay the person flat on their back on a firm surface.
Kneel beside the chest; place the heel of one hand on the center of the chest (lower half of sternum).
Place your other hand on top, interlace fingers, and keep fingers off the ribs.
Keep arms straight; compress the chest at least 2 inches (5 cm) deep at a rate of 100–120 compressions per minute.
Allow the chest to fully recoil between compressions — do not lean on the chest.
After 30 compressions, open the airway: tilt the head back and lift the chin.
Pinch the nose shut, create a seal over the mouth, and give 2 rescue breaths each lasting about 1 second — look for chest rise.
Continue cycles of 30 compressions then 2 breaths until an AED is ready, the person shows signs of life, or EMS takes over.
If you are untrained or unwilling to give rescue breaths, perform compression-only CPR at 100–120 per minute continuously.
child: Confirm the scene is safe; check the child — tap and shout. Call 911 after about 2 minutes of CPR if alone.
Lay the child flat on their back on a firm surface.
Place the heel of one hand (or two hands for a larger child) on the lower half of the sternum.
Compress the chest about 2 inches (5 cm) — roughly one-third the chest depth — at 100–120 per minute.
Allow full chest recoil between compressions.
After 30 compressions (15 if two rescuers), open the airway: tilt head back, lift chin.
Pinch the nose, seal over the mouth, and give 2 gentle rescue breaths — just enough to see the chest rise.
Continue 30:2 cycles (or 15:2 with two rescuers). Use a pediatric AED pad set if available.
Continue until the child shows signs of life, an AED is ready, or EMS arrives.
infant: Confirm scene safety; tap the foot and shout. Call 911 after 2 minutes of CPR if you are alone.
Place the infant on a firm, flat surface.
Use two fingers (middle and ring) on the center of the chest, just below the nipple line.
Compress about 1.5 inches (4 cm) — roughly one-third the chest depth — at 100–120 per minute.
Allow full chest recoil between compressions.
After 30 compressions (15 if two rescuers), tilt the head back slightly (neutral position — do not hyperextend).
Cover the infant's mouth AND nose with your mouth and give 2 small puffs lasting 1 second each — just enough to see the chest rise.
Use an infant AED pad set if available. Continue until the infant shows signs of life or EMS arrives.

fast.F: FACE: Ask the person to smile — check for drooping or unevenness on one side of the face.
fast.A: ARMS: Ask the person to raise both arms — watch for one arm that drifts downward or cannot be raised.
fast.S: SPEECH: Ask the person to repeat a simple phrase (e.g., 'The sky is blue') — listen for slurred, strange, or absent speech.
fast.T: TIME: If ANY of the above signs are present, call 911 immediately and note the exact time symptoms started — do not wait to see if symptoms improve.

tourniquet: Expose the wound by cutting away clothing if needed; do NOT remove impaled objects.
Apply direct firm pressure with a clean cloth first to assess severity.
If bleeding is life-threatening and does not stop with pressure, apply a tourniquet 2–3 inches (5–7 cm) above the wound — never over a joint.
Use a commercial tourniquet (CAT or SOFTT-W) or improvise with a strip of cloth at least 2 inches (5 cm) wide — never wire or cord.
Tighten the tourniquet until the bleeding stops — this will be painful; tightness is necessary.
Note the exact time of application (write directly on the skin with a marker if possible).
Do NOT remove the tourniquet once applied — leave it for EMS personnel.
Keep the limb elevated if possible; keep the person warm and still.
Call 911 immediately if not already done; monitor for signs of shock (pale, cold, rapid breathing, confusion).
epipen: Confirm signs of anaphylaxis: hives, swelling of face/throat, difficulty breathing, drop in blood pressure, rapid pulse — act immediately.
Call 911 before or immediately after administering epinephrine.
Remove the EpiPen from its case; grip firmly in your dominant hand, blue safety cap up, orange tip pointing down.
Pull off the blue safety release cap with your other hand.
Hold the orange tip close to the outer mid-thigh (can inject through clothing).
Swing and push the orange tip firmly against the thigh until you hear a click — hold in place for 10 seconds.
Remove the pen and massage the injection site for 10 seconds.
Note the time of injection; a second dose may be given after 5–15 minutes if symptoms do not improve and EMS has not arrived.
Lay the person down with legs elevated unless breathing is difficult — then keep them sitting upright.
Even if the person feels better, transport to emergency care immediately — biphasic reactions can occur hours later.
Give the used EpiPen to EMS personnel on arrival.

adult: Ask 'Are you choking?' — if the person cannot speak, cough, or breathe, act immediately; call 911.
Stand behind the person; place one foot forward for stability.
Make a fist with one hand and place the thumb side against the abdomen, just above the navel and well below the breastbone.
Grasp your fist firmly with the other hand.
Give a quick, firm inward-and-upward thrust — repeat up to 5 times.
Alternate 5 back blows (firm strikes between shoulder blades with the heel of your hand) with 5 abdominal thrusts if the object is not expelled.
Continue alternating until the object is expelled or the person loses consciousness.
If the person becomes unconscious, lower them to the ground carefully, call 911, and begin CPR — each time you open the airway to give breaths, look for and remove any visible object.
infant: Confirm the infant cannot cry, cough, or breathe — call 911 immediately.
Hold the infant face-down along your forearm, supporting the head; keep head lower than chest.
Give 5 firm back blows between the shoulder blades using the heel of your hand.
Flip the infant face-up on your other forearm, still supporting the head below chest level.
Place two fingers on the center of the chest just below the nipple line.
Give 5 quick downward chest thrusts about 1.5 inches deep.
Alternate 5 back blows and 5 chest thrusts; check the mouth after each set and remove any visible object — do NOT perform blind finger sweeps.
If the infant becomes unconscious, begin infant CPR and look for the object each time you open the airway to give breaths.
Continue until the object is expelled, the infant starts breathing, or EMS arrives.
